name: Honda Insight
engine: 1.5L Atkinson-cycle I4 (107 hp) + two-motor hybrid system
transmission: e-CVT (Honda two-motor hybrid, single fixed drive)
reliability: 9/10 — the third-gen Insight is a Civic underneath running Honda's proven two-motor hybrid system, the same architecture as the Accord and CR-V hybrids. There's no traditional transmission or belt to wear out, the gas engine mostly acts as a generator, and the high-voltage battery is well-proven and long-warrantied. Its NHTSA complaint count is low. The main things to know are shared Honda-era recalls (the Denso fuel pump campaign) rather than anything specific to the hybrid system.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Low · Maintenance ~$400/yr (regen braking means pads and rotors last a long time) · Excellent fuel economy · Hybrid battery warranty 8yr/100k (10yr/150k in CARB states) · Quietly cheap to run
popular_mods: All-weather floor liners — it's a premium commuter, keep the interior nice
A second set of wheels with winter tires — the best real-world upgrade for a FWD hybrid
Better wiper blades and a good cabin filter — small comfort wins on a car built for quiet miles
Honest take: the Insight is an efficiency and refinement play, not a mod platform — the smartest 'upgrade' is just driving it gently and banking the MPG

generations.1st Gen ZE1 (2000–2006 US): The frugality icon — a lightweight aluminum two-seater with a 1.0L three-cylinder IMA hybrid (~70 hp) that returned up to ~70 mpg highway. Slow and spartan, but a genuine cult classic for hypermilers.
generations.2nd Gen ZE2 (2010–2014 US): A five-door hatchback with a 1.3L IMA hybrid (~98 hp combined), pitched as a budget Prius rival. Practical and cheap, but the least loved Insight — noisier and less efficient than the Toyota.
generations.3rd Gen ZE4 (2019–2022 US): The best Insight by far — a Civic-based sedan with the two-motor hybrid (151 hp combined) and ~52 mpg combined, styled and trimmed like a premium compact rather than an eco-appliance. Effectively a stealth hybrid Civic. Discontinued after 2022 as Honda folded the hybrid back into the Civic and Accord lineups.

common_issues: Denso low-pressure fuel pump recall — a widespread 2018–2020 Honda/Acura campaign; verify by VIN it's been done
12V (accessory) battery can drain if the car sits for long stretches — a common hybrid quirk, not a fault; a trickle charger fixes it
Road noise on the highway, firmer ride on the Touring's larger wheels — comfort notes, not defects
Trunk-only cargo (the battery lives behind the seat), so it's less versatile than a hatchback
Real-world MPG dips noticeably in cold weather — true of every hybrid, worth knowing before you buy
maintenance_tips: Change the 0W-20 oil on the Maintenance Minder — the Atkinson engine runs easy and lasts
Regenerative braking means the friction brakes barely wear — but still flush the brake fluid on schedule and exercise the calipers so they don't seize
If the car will sit for weeks, put the 12V battery on a maintainer — it prevents the most common no-start complaint
Confirm the Denso fuel pump recall and any other open campaigns by VIN before buying
There's no CVT belt or timing belt to service — this is a genuinely low-maintenance drivetrain
